claim: Kant's Categorical Imperative requires acting only on maxims that can be willed as universal law
quote: Act only according to that maxim whereby you can at the same time will that it should become a universal law

claim: Lying fails the universalizability test by generating a contradiction in conception
quote: If everyone lied all the time, then no one would ever be able to trust anyone else

claim: A lie is an intentionally deceptive assertion
quote: an intentionally deceptive message in the form of a statement

claim: Augustine and Aquinas argued lying is always impermissible
quote: Aquinas takes there to be an absolute prohibition against lying

claim: Aquinas distinguishes lying from intentional deceit
quote: Aquinas rejects the view that lying essentially involves any form of intentional deceit

claim: Universalizability requires maxims be willable as universal law without contradiction
quote: requires that the maxim be capable of being willed as a universal law without generating a logical contradiction

claim: Kantian deontology grounds morality in duty and the categorical imperative
quote: the only thing that is unconditionally good is a good will

claim: Plato introduced the concept of the noble lie in the Republic
quote: a foundational myth proposed in Book III of Plato's Republic

claim: Kant argued there is no right to lie even to a murderer at the door
quote: a lie always harms another; if not some human being, then it nevertheless does harm to humanity in general

claim: Radical honesty is the practice of complete honesty without white lies
quote: the practice of complete honesty without telling even white lies

claim: Kantian self-preservation duty can require lying
quote: our perfect Kantian duty of self-preservation can require our lying to save our own lives

claim: Lying destroys the moral Denkungsart in Kant's framework
quote: being truthful is a condition and an integral part of the moral Denkungsart
